headline: ACREAGE DAY, HORMUZ FLARES, SOYOIL RUNS 6.9%
subheadline: The USDA Acreage report lands at 11 AM CT Tuesday; overnight, fresh U.S.-Iran strikes rattled Hormuz tanker traffic and soybean oil had its biggest session in weeks.
lead: Tuesday's Acreage report is the only thing that matters this week, and the market spent Monday telling you exactly how nervous it is. Corn nearby dropped 10.5 cents to $4.13 while December barely budged at $4.42, a split that says old-crop fear is running hot but the new-crop trade is waiting for data before it commits. Meanwhile, soybean oil ran 6.9% to $71.30 on fresh U.S.-Iran strikes this weekend that slowed tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz again, and the board opens Tuesday carrying two live grenades: one from the policy side, one from the weather map with a heat dome building over the Belt.
the_takeaway: Get to Tuesday's 11 AM Acreage number before you price anything new-crop.
teaser: Acreage Tuesday, Hormuz flares again, soyoil runs hard. Here's what it means before the bell.

one_number.unit: share of U.S. soybeans now crushed domestically
one_number.context: Three or four years ago, over 60% of U.S. soybeans were exported. The National Oilseed Processors Association says that ratio has now flipped, with domestic crush absorbing the majority. That structural shift is exactly why a Hormuz-driven soyoil rally hits the board this hard: the domestic biofuel demand chain is now the primary customer, not a Chinese cargo ship. When Hormuz rattles palm oil supplies, domestic soyoil fills the gap and the futures board reprices it immediately.

yesterdays_call.note: November beans closed at $11.56, right at the called level, but the direction was up from $11.26 nearby -- and the computed outcome is 'didnt', meaning the specific threshold was not cleared with enough margin to score as played out. The Hormuz soyoil rally added 6.9% to soyoil and firmed November beans 6.75 cents on the session, so the directional read was right but the beans call did not fully deliver at the level. Own the miss and move on -- the new-crop bean trade is still the one to watch into Tuesday's Acreage print.

sections.title: CORN BREAKS HARD BEFORE ACREAGE
sections.body: Corn nearby dropped 10.5 cents to **$4.13** and that move is almost entirely pre-Acreage positioning. December held at $4.42, off just a penny, and that split is the tell: the funds are lightening old-crop exposure ahead of Tuesday's 11 AM print, not making a directional call on the new crop. The Acreage report could show fewer planted acres than the March estimate, which would add premium to December, or it could confirm the big number that the wet-May planting pace suggested. Iowa and Missouri farmers caught dry windows between rain systems and pushed corn planting hard through late May, so a number above 94 million acres is very much in play. If Tuesday comes in above 94 million, the December contract at $4.42 is the first line the market tests lower. If it comes in below 93 million, watch for the new-crop to run back toward $4.55 in a hurry. A North Iowa grain farmer told Brownfield last week there are 'few if any pathways to profitability this year.' At $4.13 nearby, he is not wrong.
sections.catalyst: Pre-Acreage fund liquidation in old-crop; USDA Acreage report due 11 AM CT Tuesday.
sections.bottom_line: Old-crop fear is priced; new-crop waits on Tuesday's acre count.
sections.farmer_action: If you have unpriced old-crop corn in storage and local basis is firm, price today. Do not wait through the Acreage print with unhedged bushels.

sections.title: SOYOIL RUNS; HORMUZ FLARES AGAIN
sections.body: Soybean oil had its biggest single session in weeks, running 6.9% to **$71.30** after fresh U.S.-Iran strikes this weekend slowed tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, the Iran-Hormuz tensions that built since early April and briefly appeared to be deflating on diplomatic progress have reignited sharply. Pakistan's state LNG importer was out seeking emergency cargo as of Monday morning and vessel owners are pulling back from Hormuz transits. That tightens global vegetable oil flows and domestic soyoil fills the gap fast. Soybean meal also firmed, up 1.3% to **$307.00**, so the crush is running both sides of the board higher. Soybeans nearby gave back 23 cents to $11.26 on old-crop selling, but November beans actually added 6.75 cents to **$11.56**: the market is telling you the new-crop demand picture looks better than the old-crop carry. The domestic biofuel shift matters here. With roughly 60% of U.S. soybeans now going to domestic crush rather than export, a Hormuz disruption that tightens palm oil supply hits the soyoil board directly and the RFS demand base amplifies it.
sections.catalyst: Weekend U.S.-Iran strikes; Strait of Hormuz tanker traffic slowing per oilprice.com; domestic soyoil demand base from RFS finalized RVOs.
sections.bottom_line: Soyoil is the Hormuz trade right now; November beans are acting better than nearby.
sections.farmer_action: Producers holding unpriced new-crop beans: the November contract at $11.56 is the cleanest level of the week. Consider layering in sales on any Tuesday pop from a friendly Acreage number before the heat dome story fully prices.

sections.title: WHEAT FALLS; OATS HIT AGAIN
sections.body: Chicago wheat dropped 17.5 cents to **$5.78**, its third week of pressure, and there is no clean catalyst beyond the broader grain complex selling ahead of Tuesday. The Middle East production rebound to between 14.6 and 15 million barrels per day of oil, combined with the Hormuz flare, has investors weighing energy over wheat fundamentals today. The real story in this bucket is oats, which had a 15.1% single-session drop to **$2.77**, now sitting at just 8% above its 52-week low. Oats have now posted double-digit percentage losses on multiple sessions in the past week. The fund exit in oats looks nearly complete on a positioning basis, but 'nearly complete' does not mean the floor is in. If you have oats in storage, the futures price alone is not giving you a reason to hold.
sections.catalyst: Broad pre-Acreage grain selling; oats fund liquidation ongoing per multi-session pattern.
sections.bottom_line: Wheat lacks a fresh catalyst; oats are near 52-week lows with no visible floor.
sections.farmer_action: If you have unpriced oats in storage, compare today's local basis against cost of carry. The futures contract at $2.77 is not building a case for patience.

sections.title: CATTLE STEADY; HOGS FALL HARD
sections.body: Live cattle eased to **$245.82**, off 0.6%, and feeders gave back 0.9% to **$369.85**. The ongoing Cargill Fort Morgan/Schuyler plant lockout that began May 19 has kept the fed cattle trade in a processing-constrained posture for six weeks now, and the market is not finding new sellers at these levels even with box beef softer. The cattle complex is not acting healthy, but it is not breaking either. Hogs are a different story. Lean hogs dropped 3.9% to **$92.92**, their sharpest single-session move in recent weeks. Hog markets have been drifting and today's break is the kind of move that happens when the funds run out of reasons to stay long in a seasonally weak period without a fresh demand catalyst. Class III Milk also fell 2.5% to **$16.01**, consistent with its multi-week drift lower. No single news item drove hogs today; this looks like fund liquidation in a market that has been losing conviction for two weeks.
sections.catalyst: Cargill Fort Morgan lockout ongoing, processing-constrained cattle dynamics; hog fund liquidation with no fresh demand catalyst.
sections.bottom_line: Cattle holding but not healthy; hogs broke hard on fund exit, not a news event.

sections.title: ENERGY PULLS BACK; HORMUZ PREMIUM RETURNS
sections.body: WTI crude fell 1.9% to **$69.23** even as the Strait of Hormuz situation re-escalated this weekend. That divergence is worth reading carefully: crude lost roughly 19% during May as the Hormuz premium unwound on diplomatic progress, and Monday's fresh U.S.-Iran strikes brought the premium back, but the crude market's reaction was muted while soyoil ran hard. Middle East oil production rebounding to between 14.6 and 15 million barrels per day is the offset: more supply is coming to market even as the transit risk returns. Natural gas eased 1.6% to **$3.23**. The LNG side of the story is louder: Pakistan sought emergency cargo Monday morning, and China's June LNG imports are running above May levels on summer power demand. The $5.5 billion Williams/Momentum Midstream deal in late-stage talks signals the midstream sector still sees long-term domestic gas infrastructure as a growth trade regardless of near-term price softness.
sections.catalyst: Weekend U.S.-Iran strikes; Middle East production rebound to 14.6-15 million bpd per Financial Express; Pakistan emergency LNG procurement.
sections.bottom_line: Crude muted despite Hormuz flare; the soyoil market read the geopolitics faster than crude did.

outside_the_pit.title: New Screwworm Cases Threatening Beef Production, Pricing
outside_the_pit.body: Congressional Democrats sent a letter to USDA Secretary Rollins demanding transparency and science-driven action on the New World Screwworm as cases push closer to U.S. cattle territory. The livestock disease is a long-tail supply risk that the cattle complex has not begun to price, and the processing-constrained dynamics from the Cargill lockout mean the industry has little buffer if screwworm containment fails.

outside_the_pit.title: New York and Connecticut Launch State-Level Tariff Relief for Farmers
outside_the_pit.body: New York and Connecticut became the first states to open applications for direct tariff relief to farmers, with New York offering up to $25,000 per producer from a $30 million state fund. This is a state-level patch on a federal problem: the North Dakota State study estimated active China tariffs cost U.S. ag $15 billion annually, and no state fund closes that gap, but it is the first visible political response below the federal level.

outside_the_pit.title: K-State Expands Corn Leafhopper Monitoring After Oklahoma Detections
outside_the_pit.body: Kansas Corn and Kansas State University are deploying expanded monitoring and free diagnostic testing statewide after corn leafhopper detections in Oklahoma this season. Leafhopper pressure during late vegetative and early reproductive stages can accelerate corn streak mosaic virus spread. With pollination approaching across the Belt, this is worth watching if the pressure moves north.

spread_to_watch.label: Soybeans nearby / November beans inverse
spread_to_watch.commentary: July beans at $11.26 and November at $11.56 means new crop is trading $0.30 above old crop, and that inverse widened today as old-crop sold off while November added. That is the market pricing better new-crop demand, driven by the domestic crush shift and the Hormuz soyoil story, while old-crop carry is being discarded ahead of the Acreage print. Watch whether Tuesday's Acreage number narrows or widens this spread: a bullish (low-acre) number closes the gap; a bearish (high-acre) number blows it wider.

basis.headline: Old-crop corn basis firming; soybean basis mixed ahead of Acreage.
basis.body: Eastern Belt corn basis is **firming** as pre-Acreage selling hits the futures board harder than cash markets, creating a window for producers with old-crop in storage who have firm local bids. Western Belt basis remains soft, no change from last week's pattern. Soybean basis is mixed: the soyoil-driven strength in new-crop futures is not fully translating to old-crop cash bids, and merchandisers are sitting on their hands waiting for Tuesday's acre count before they move basis aggressively either direction.

weekly_thread.question: Does Tuesday's Acreage report reset the corn price floor above $4.25, or does the acre count confirm the bear case and break new-crop lower?
weekly_thread.status_text: The entire grain complex is coiled around the 11 AM CT Tuesday Acreage print. Corn nearby is already at $4.13 with old-crop selling into the report. The question for the week is whether the planted-acre number gives new-crop corn a reason to recover toward $4.55 or confirms the supply is big enough to keep the bear in charge.

the_more_you_know.title: The heat dome arriving this week is a pollination clock, not just a weather headline.
the_more_you_know.body: With corn nearby at $4.13 and no weather premium in the December contract at $4.42, the market is not yet pricing the heat dome building over the Belt. Here is why that could change fast: corn is a narrow-window crop at pollination. A sustained stretch above 95 degrees Fahrenheit during silking, roughly a 10-to-14-day window that varies by planting date and geography, can cut yield by 3% to 8% per day of heat stress. The 2026 planting season ran behind the average through May before catching up between rain systems, which means the Belt's pollination window is slightly later than average this year. That delay puts peak pollination exposure squarely in early-to-mid July, right when this heat dome is forecast to arrive. If the heat dome stalls rather than moves through, the December contract at $4.42 will not stay quiet for long. Scouts and agronomists should be checking silk emergence dates this week. The Acreage number may reprice supply; the weather map may reprice yield. Both could move the board in the same week.

watch_list.desc: USDA Acreage Report: corn acres above 94 million pushes December toward $4.30; below 93 million adds weather premium and targets $4.55.
USDA Crop Progress: corn condition rated good-to-excellent above 65% keeps the heat premium in check; below 60% starts the weather conversation early.
Weekly Export Sales: soybean sales above 400K MT confirm the new-crop demand story; below 250K MT and November beans at $11.56 are running on Hormuz momentum alone.
Strait of Hormuz: any fresh escalation beyond weekend's U.S.-Iran strikes keeps soyoil bid; a return to Swiss-channel diplomacy deflates the premium that ran 6.9% today.
Heat dome track: if the dome stalls over the Corn Belt with pollination approaching, watch December corn at $4.42 for the first sign of weather premium.

daily_quote.text: Great oaks from little acorns grow.

source_summary: USDA NASS, CME Group, Brownfield Ag News, oilprice.com, farmpolicynews.illinois.edu, feedstuffs.com, farmdocdaily.illinois.edu, beefmagazine.com, thefencepost.com
